title: Strings
description: Master C# string operations: indexing, Substring, Split/Join, StringBuilder, and immutability for coding interviews.

sections.title: String Indexing and Properties
sections.explanation: Strings in C# are immutable sequences of `char`. Access characters with `s[i]` (zero-indexed). Get length with `.Length`. Use `.IndexOf()` to find positions, `.Contains()` for existence checks. `char` is a value type—compare with `==`. Strings support slicing via `Substring()` but not negative indexing like Python.

exercise.prompt: Given string s = "programming", print: the first character, the last character, the length, the index of 'g' (first occurrence), and whether it contains "gram".

validation.message: First: p, Last: g, Length: 11, IndexOf 'g': 3, Contains "gram": True.

exercise.hint: s[0] for first, s[s.Length - 1] for last. IndexOf('g') returns 3. Contains("gram") returns True.

sections.title: Substring and Manipulation
sections.explanation: `Substring(start)` returns from start to end. `Substring(start, length)` returns `length` characters from `start`. Note: the second parameter is length, not end index (unlike Java). Other common methods: `ToUpper()`, `ToLower()`, `Trim()`, `Replace()`, `StartsWith()`, `EndsWith()`. All return new strings since strings are immutable.

exercise.prompt: Given string s = "  Data Structures  ", trim it and store in `trimmed`. Print trimmed. Extract "Data" using Substring (starts at index 0, length 4 of trimmed). Print it uppercased. Replace "Structures" with "Algorithms" in trimmed and print the result.

validation.message: Trimmed string, DATA in uppercase, then replaced string.
Use Trim, Substring, ToUpper, and Replace methods.

exercise.hint: s.Trim() removes whitespace. Substring(0, 4) gets "Data". ToUpper() makes it "DATA". Replace swaps substrings.

sections.title: Split and Join
sections.explanation: `Split(char)` or `Split(string)` breaks a string into a `string[]`. `String.Join(separator, array)` combines an array into one string. These are essential for parsing input and formatting output in interviews. `Split` can take `StringSplitOptions.RemoveEmptyEntries` to skip empty results from consecutive delimiters.

exercise.prompt: Given string sentence = "the quick brown fox", split it by spaces into an array. Print the number of words and the third word. Reverse the array using Array.Reverse, then join with "-" and print the result.

validation.message: 4 words, third word is brown, reversed and joined with dashes.
Use Split to tokenize, Array.Reverse, and String.Join.

exercise.hint: Split(' ') gives array of words. words[2] is "brown". Array.Reverse mutates in-place. String.Join("-", words).

sections.title: StringBuilder for Efficient Concatenation
sections.explanation: Since strings are immutable, concatenation in a loop creates O(n) new strings, making it O(n²). Use `StringBuilder` from `System.Text` for O(n) string building. Key methods: `Append()`, `Insert()`, `Remove()`, `Replace()`, `ToString()`. In interviews, always use StringBuilder when building strings character-by-character in a loop.

exercise.prompt: Use StringBuilder to build a comma-separated string of squares from 1 to 5: "1,4,9,16,25". Don't include a trailing comma. Print the result.

validation.message: Use StringBuilder with Append for efficient string building.

exercise.hint: Loop i from 1 to 5. Append i*i. Append comma only if i < 5, or use sb.Length check.

sections.title: String Immutability and Comparison
sections.explanation: Strings in C# are immutable—every modification returns a new string. Use `==` for content comparison (C# overloads `==` for strings, unlike Java). `String.Equals()` also works, and supports `StringComparison` options for case-insensitive comparison. `string.Compare()` returns -1, 0, or 1 for ordering. Converting to char array with `ToCharArray()` lets you mutate, then create a new string.

exercise.prompt: Given string s = "Interview", convert to a char array, reverse it, and create a new string. Print the reversed string. Then compare "abc" and "ABC" using == (case sensitive) and Equals with OrdinalIgnoreCase. Print both results.

validation.message: Reversed string, then False for == and True for case-insensitive Equals.
Use ToCharArray, Array.Reverse, new string(), and StringComparison.OrdinalIgnoreCase.

exercise.hint: ToCharArray() gives char[], Array.Reverse mutates it, new string(chars) builds the reversed string.
